title: Project Initiation
description: Advanced quiz on project selection, business cases, project charters, governance, sponsorship, benefits, assumptions and early-stage decision-making.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the project initiation phase?
options: To create a detailed activity schedule
To determine whether a proposed project is justified and should be formally authorized
To assign every team member to a work package
To negotiate all supplier contracts
questions.explanation: Initiation evaluates whether the project is worthwhile and establishes formal authorization. Detailed scheduling, work allocation and contracting normally occur later.

questions.question: Which document normally provides formal authorization for a project to begin?
options: Project Charter
Stakeholder Register
Work Breakdown Structure
Procurement Schedule
questions.explanation: The Project Charter formally authorizes the project and typically confirms the project purpose, high-level scope, sponsor and project manager.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a Business Case?
options: To describe why the project should be undertaken and what value it is expected to create
To provide detailed technical specifications for every deliverable
To record the daily decisions of the project team
To replace the final project plan
questions.explanation: The Business Case explains the rationale for the project, including the problem, expected benefits, costs, risks and alternatives.

questions.question: Who normally owns the Business Case and remains accountable for the project's continued business justification?
options: Project Sponsor
Project Scheduler
Technical Lead
Procurement Officer
questions.explanation: The sponsor normally owns the Business Case and ensures that the project continues to support organizational objectives.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes project initiation from project planning?
options: Initiation determines why and whether the project should proceed, while planning determines how it will be delivered
Initiation creates the full schedule, while planning approves the project
Initiation is only financial, while planning is only technical
There is no meaningful difference between the two phases
questions.explanation: Initiation concerns justification, alignment and authorization. Planning develops the detailed roadmap for execution.

questions.question: Why should strategic alignment be assessed before a project is approved?
options: Because strategically aligned projects never experience risk
Because a project can be delivered efficiently yet still create little organizational value
Because strategy determines the exact project schedule
Because only strategic projects require sponsors
questions.explanation: A project may meet time, cost and scope targets but still be a poor investment if it does not support the organization's priorities.

questions.question: Why is a high-level scope included in the Project Charter rather than a fully detailed scope statement?
options: Because no scope information is available before execution
Because initiation defines boundaries and intent, while detailed decomposition belongs to planning
Because detailed scope is only required for construction projects
Because the sponsor should not know what the project will deliver
questions.explanation: The charter provides enough scope clarity to authorize the project without attempting to complete detailed planning prematurely.

questions.question: Why should alternatives be considered in a Business Case?
options: To demonstrate that the preferred proposal is better than other credible ways of addressing the need
To delay the approval decision
To ensure that every alternative is implemented
To eliminate the need for risk analysis
questions.explanation: A strong Business Case compares the proposed project with realistic alternatives, including doing nothing, improving existing processes or selecting another solution.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between an assumption and a constraint during initiation?
options: An assumption is treated as true for planning purposes, while a constraint limits available choices
An assumption is mandatory, while a constraint is optional
An assumption is always financial, while a constraint is always technical
There is no practical distinction
questions.explanation: Assumptions are uncertain conditions temporarily treated as true. Constraints impose limits, such as fixed deadlines, budgets or regulatory requirements.

questions.question: Why is early identification of major risks important even before detailed planning begins?
options: Because all project risks must be eliminated during initiation
Because major risks may change whether the project should be approved at all
Because detailed risk responses must be executed immediately
Because project risks only affect the sponsor
questions.explanation: Some risks are significant enough to affect feasibility, expected value or strategic acceptability and must therefore inform the approval decision.

questions.question: Why should success criteria be defined during initiation?
options: To create a basis for judging whether the project eventually delivers its intended value
To guarantee that the schedule will not change
To replace stakeholder analysis
To determine individual employee salaries
questions.explanation: Success criteria clarify what outcomes matter and create an early reference point for evaluating project performance and benefits.

questions.question: Why is appointing a project sponsor insufficient unless the sponsor is actively engaged?
options: Because sponsorship is primarily a ceremonial role
Because the project may still lack timely decisions, organizational support and access to resources
Because only project managers may make decisions
Because sponsors are responsible for completing technical tasks
questions.explanation: Effective sponsorship requires active ownership, decision-making, escalation support and protection of the project's business rationale.

questions.question: Why can a technically feasible project still be rejected during initiation?
options: Because technical feasibility is irrelevant
Because feasibility alone does not prove that the project is strategically valuable, affordable or preferable to alternatives
Because all technically feasible projects must be delayed
Because only non-technical projects require approval
questions.explanation: Technical feasibility is only one decision criterion. Strategic fit, financial value, risk and organizational capacity also matter.

questions.question: A shipping company is considering a predictive-maintenance platform. The software is technically strong, but the company has no reliable equipment data. What should management do during initiation?
options: Approve the project because the technology is advanced
Ignore the data issue until implementation
Assess whether the data limitation undermines feasibility, benefits and expected value
Ask the vendor to take full ownership of the Business Case
questions.explanation: The quality and availability of data are critical assumptions. Management should assess whether the expected benefits remain credible before authorization.

questions.question: A company wants to build a new warehouse because its existing facility is congested. Which initiation activity should occur before selecting the construction option?
options: Compare alternatives such as process redesign, outsourcing, expansion or new construction
Appoint the construction contractor
Create the final building schedule
Purchase the land immediately
questions.explanation: The organization should first confirm that new construction is the best response to the underlying business problem.

questions.question: A project is expected to save USD 2 million annually, but the estimate assumes full employee adoption from the first day. What is the most appropriate initiation response?
options: Treat the savings as guaranteed
Document the adoption assumption and test how sensitive the Business Case is to lower adoption
Remove the benefit estimate entirely
Approve the project because savings exceed zero
questions.explanation: Important assumptions should be made explicit and tested. Benefits that depend on behavior or adoption are rarely guaranteed.

questions.question: Senior management has verbally approved a project, but no charter exists and departments disagree about whether resources may be assigned. What is the best next step?
options: Begin execution to maintain momentum
Create formal authorization clarifying authority, ownership, objectives and high-level boundaries
Let each department interpret the approval independently
Ask suppliers to begin work at their own risk
questions.explanation: A formal charter resolves ambiguity by documenting authorization, governance and the project manager's mandate.

questions.question: A port automation project supports the company's strategy, but it requires scarce engineers who are already assigned to a regulatory compliance project. What should be evaluated before approval?
options: Only the financial return of the automation project
The organization's capacity and the opportunity cost of diverting scarce resources
Whether the automation vendor offers training
Whether the project manager prefers the project
questions.explanation: Project selection must consider organizational capacity and competing priorities, not only the attractiveness of the project in isolation.

questions.question: A sponsor asks the project manager to produce a detailed 18-month schedule before the project has passed the feasibility review. What is the most professional response?
options: Refuse to provide any timing information
Develop a high-level estimate while explaining that detailed planning depends on approval and clearer scope
Create a fully committed schedule immediately
Delegate the decision to the supplier
questions.explanation: A high-level estimate may support decision-making, but detailed commitments should not be presented as reliable before feasibility and scope are sufficiently understood.

questions.question: A project is proposed mainly because a senior executive is enthusiastic about a new technology. Which initiation question is most important?
options: Which technology brand does the executive prefer?
What business problem will the project solve and what measurable benefits are expected?
How quickly can the project team begin?
Which department will receive the largest budget?
questions.explanation: Technology enthusiasm is not a sufficient rationale. The project must be connected to a defined need and credible benefits.

questions.question: A proposed ERP project has strong benefits, but several previous transformation projects failed because departments resisted standardized processes. What should the Business Case include?
options: Only the software acquisition cost
Organizational change risks, adoption requirements and the resources needed to realize benefits
A statement that resistance will disappear after launch
A fully detailed technical design
questions.explanation: The Business Case should reflect the full conditions required to deliver benefits, including organizational change and adoption.

questions.question: A government regulation will require new emissions-reporting capability within twelve months. The project has low direct financial return. What is the strongest justification for approval?
options: The project creates optional product features
The project prevents non-compliance and protects the organization's ability to operate
The project will improve employee morale
The project manager has available capacity
questions.explanation: Mandatory compliance projects may be justified through avoided penalties, operating continuity and legal obligations rather than direct financial return.

questions.question: A project idea has attractive benefits, but the estimated costs are based on informal vendor conversations and omit internal labor. What should happen before approval?
options: Approve it because the benefits are attractive
Improve the cost estimate and include all material cost categories
Transfer all estimating responsibility to the project manager after approval
Remove costs from the Business Case
questions.explanation: An approval decision based on materially incomplete costs may overstate project value. The estimate should include external and internal resource requirements.

questions.question: A sponsor insists that a project must be approved because a competitor has launched a similar service. What should the initiation team do first?
options: Copy the competitor's solution
Assess whether the competitor's action changes the organization's own strategic need, customer demand and expected value
Approve the project immediately to avoid delay
Reject the project because imitation is always poor strategy
questions.explanation: Competitor behavior is relevant evidence, but it does not replace analysis of the organization's own strategy, customers, capabilities and economics.

questions.question: Two projects have similar financial returns. Project A supports a core strategic priority but has high implementation risk. Project B has lower risk but limited strategic relevance. What is the best conclusion?
options: Project A should automatically be selected
Project B should automatically be selected
The choice requires an explicit evaluation of strategic value, risk tolerance and portfolio priorities
Both projects should be approved because their returns are similar
questions.explanation: Project selection requires balanced judgment. Neither strategic alignment nor low risk should be treated as the only criterion.

questions.question: A project's Business Case relies heavily on benefits that will occur five years after implementation. Which concern deserves the greatest attention?
options: Long-term benefits are always invalid
The further into the future benefits occur, the greater the uncertainty around assumptions, realization and strategic relevance
The project should have no sponsor
The detailed schedule must cover all five years
questions.explanation: Long-dated benefits can be legitimate, but they require careful treatment because uncertainty, organizational change and shifting priorities increase over time.

questions.question: A project has been formally approved, but the charter describes success only as 'implement the new system.' What is the most significant weakness?
options: The charter contains too much detail
The project has defined an output but not the intended outcome or business value
The project manager has too much authority
The sponsor should be removed
questions.explanation: Delivering a system is an output. Strong initiation also defines the outcomes and benefits the system is expected to enable.

questions.question: A project appears financially attractive, but success depends on three departments changing established procedures. None of the department heads were involved in the Business Case. What is the most serious issue?
options: The project lacks technical feasibility
The benefit assumptions may not be credible because key benefit owners were not engaged
The charter should contain a detailed schedule
The project manager should personally own all benefits
questions.explanation: Benefits often depend on operational changes outside the project team's direct control. Relevant managers should validate assumptions and accept ownership.

questions.question: A project is approved because its financial model shows a positive return. Six months later, the underlying market demand has fallen significantly. What governance principle is most relevant?
options: Once approved, a project should never be reconsidered
Business justification should be reviewed when material assumptions change
Only technical risks justify reassessment
The project manager should hide the change to protect team morale
questions.explanation: Authorization is not permanent proof of value. Projects should be challenged when major assumptions, risks or strategic conditions change.

questions.question: A sponsor argues that uncertainty should not delay authorization because detailed planning will eventually resolve all open questions. What is the most accurate response?
options: All uncertainty must be eliminated before approval
Projects should be approved whenever a sponsor is confident
Initiation should reduce uncertainty enough to support a responsible decision, while acknowledging that some uncertainty will remain
Detailed planning makes the Business Case unnecessary
questions.explanation: Initiation does not require certainty. It requires sufficient evidence, transparency and risk understanding to make a defensible decision about whether to proceed.
